data.compiling-source-files#Compiling Source:
Once you have written your source file (ex. file-name.daisy), you can compile it like this:
daisy file-name
This will look for a file file-name with an extension .daisy, in the current directory.
data.waching-source-files#Waching Source File:
When you're making many rapid changes to a source file or it's imports it can be tedious to constantly have to re-run the build command.
Daisy offers a solution in the form of a --watch option, when passed the target file and all it's imports (including imports of imports and so on) will be watched for changes recompiling automatically when one occurs.
daisy file-name --watch

data: You can download pre-built binaries directly from the GitHub Releases page.

data.writing-your-first-source-file#Writing Y:
Daisy's language is really simple, you will learn more through the guide.
First create a .daisy file in your desired directory, then copy the example below into your file:
option Outputs = {
    Components = "Path/To/Components",
    Scheduler = "Path/To/Scheduler",
    Systems = "Path/To/Systems"
    World = "Path/To/World",
    Tags = "Path/To/Tags",
}
 
component Health = u8(0..100)
tag IsPoisoned
 
system PoisonHurts = {
    Query = {mut:Health},
    With = {IsPoisoned},
    Phase = Update
}
data.compiling-your-source-file#Compiling You:
Open a terminal in the directory you created your file and run the following command:
daisy file-name
data.writing-system-logic#Writing System Logi:
--!optimize 2
--!native
--!strict

local World = require(Path/To/World)

local Components = require(Path/To/Components)
local Tags = require(Path/To/Tags)

type Mutable = {
    health: Components.Health,
}

return function(entity: World.Entity, mut: Mutable, delta_time: number)
    mut.health -= 1 * delta_time --User Written
end
data.using-the-generated-code#Using The Gener:
local ECS = path.to.ECS

local World = require(ECS.World)
local Scheduler = require(ECS.Scheduler)

local Components = require(ECS.Components)
local Tags = require(ECS.Tags)

local entity = World.Spawn()
World.Set(entity, Components.Health, 100)
World.Add(entity, Tags.IsPoisoned)

Scheduler.Start()

data: Daisy takes a simple, declarative source file and generates a fully structured ECS tailored to your project.
data.performance#Performance: Daisy aims to generate the most performant ECS for your experience, based on the components, archetypes, and systems you define.
data.get-started#Get Started: Head over to the installation page to get up and running with Daisy!

data:
A component characterizes an entity as possessing a particular quality or capability, and holds the data needed to model it.
Components are pure data, they contain no logic of their own.
data.defining-components#Defining Components:
You can define a component using the component keyword followed by the component name and its underlying data type.
component Health = u8

data:
An archetype represents a unique combination of component types.
All entities with exactly the same set of components are grouped into the same archetype.
This grouping is what allows the ECS to efficiently query and iterate over entities.
data.defining-archetypes#Defining Archetypes:
You can define an archetype using the archetype keyword followed by its name and a set of components. Components marked with ? are optional, meaning an entity in this archetype may or may not have them.
archetype Person = {Health, IsPoisoned?}

data:
Options go at the top of a source file, and allow you to configure the output of Daisy.
option [OPTION] = [VALUE]
data.output#Output:
Allows you to specify where Daisy will generate the respective output files.
option Output = {
    Archetypes = "path/to/Archetypes",
    Components = "path/to/Components",
    Scheduler = "path/to/Scheduler",
    Factory = "path/to/Factory",
    Systems = "path/to/Systems",
    World = "path/to/World",
    Tags = "path/to/Tags"
}
data.native#Native:
Wether or not to include the --!native flag at the top of compiled modules.
Default	Options	true	true, false	
option Native = false
data.strict#Strict:
Wether or not to include the --!strict flag at the top of compiled modules.
Default	Options	true	true, false	
option Strict = false

data: A system is a process that runs on all entities matching a given set of component requirements. Systems are where all of your game's logic lives — each system is responsible for one focused behavior.
data.queries#Queries: A Query defines which components a system will access on each matching entity. Only entities that satisfy the query will be processed by the system.
data.inclusionsexclusions#Inclusions/Exclusio: Systems can further filter which entities they operate on using With and Without blocks, independently of the Query.
data.with#With:
The With field ensures only entities that possess specific components or tags are included.
With = {IsPoisoned}
data.without#Without:
The Without field excludes entities that possess certain components or tags.
Without = {IsDead}
data.phases#Phases: Phases determine when a system runs each frame. If you're familiar with Roblox's RunService, you can think of phases as its events, such as Heartbeat, PreRender, or PostSimulation.
data.startup-phases#Startup Phases:
Systems assigned to Startup phases only run once, before any other systems execute.
PreStartup
Startup
PostStartup
data.runservice-phases#RunService Phases:
Each RunService event (excluding Heartbeat) corresponds to its own phase.
PreRender
PreAnimation
PreSimulation
PostSimulation
data.heartbeat-phases#Heartbeat Phases:
RunService.Heartbeat is not a single phase, it is broken into several ordered phases. Since most game logic runs here, this makes it easy to control the execution order of your systems right out of the box.
First
PreUpdate
Update
PostUpdate
Last

data:
A tag is a kind of component that carries no data, its presence on an entity alone signals that something is true about it.
Tags are useful for marking entities with a boolean state without any additional overhead.
data.defining-tags#Defining Tags:
You can define a tag using the tag keyword followed by the tag name.
tag IsPoisoned

data:
This section contains a deep dive explanation on all types supported by Daisy.
These are mostly for Networking which is not yet implemented!
data.ranges#Ranges: Ranges can be used with various types like: Numbers, Strings, Arrays etc. to limit the extents of their representable values.
data.full-ranges#Full Ranges:
A full range is one with both a minimum and maximum value.
An example full range from 0 to 100 is written like: (0..100).
data.half-ranges#Half Ranges:
A half range is one where only a minimum or maximum value is given.
These are written like: (0..) or (..100).
data.exact-ranges#Exact Ranges: An exact range has only one value, such as (0) or (100).
data.numbers#Numbers: Daisy has support for all buffer implemented number types, signed integers, unsigned integers, floating points, and more.Number types start with a prefix like: u, i or f, and are followed by the number of bits used to represent the number. The number of bits also corresponds to the cost of sending a particular number type over the network.
data.unsigned-integers#Unsigned Integers:
Unsigned integers are whole numbers, greater than or equal to zero.
Name	Size	Min	Max	u8	1 byte	0	255	u16	2 bytes	0	65,535	u32	4 bytes	0	4,294,967,295
data.signed-integers#Signed Integers:
Signed integers, unlike unsigned integers, can represent both positive and negative whole numbers.
Name	Size	Min	Max	i8	1 byte	-128	127	i16	2 bytes	-32,768	32,767	i32	4 bytes	-2,147,483,648	2,147,483,647
data.floating-points#Floating Points:
Floating points represent numbers with a decimal point.Unlike integers, the bit size of a floating point does not correlate to a hard range limit. Instead it determines its numerical accuracy (or precision). For this reason, the table below lists the maximum accurately representable integer by each floating point type.
Internally Luau stores its numbers as a f64.
The f16 type isn't natively supported by the buffer library, making it slower to read and write than other types.
Name	Size	Min	Max	f16	2 byte	-2048	2048	f32	4 bytes	-16,777,216	16,777,216	f64	8 bytes	-9,007,199,254,740,992	9,007,199,254,740,992
data.bounding-numbers#Bounding Numbers:
The values of numbers can be bounded by placing a range within parenthesis after the type.
An example health u8 number type from 0 to 100 can be written like:
u8(0..100)
u8(..100)
data.example-usage#Example Usage:
component Health = u8(0..100)
component UserId = f64
data.strings#Strings: Strings are Luau's text container, in daisy a string can be defined using the string type.
data.bounding-strings#Bounding Strings:
The length of strings can be bounded by placing a range within parenthesis after the type.
For example, if you wanted to bound a string between 3 and 20 characters you can do:
string(3..20)
data.example-usage-1#Example usage:
component UUID = string(36)
component Username = string(3..20)
data.booleans#Booleans:
Booleans are a true or false value.
They can be defined using the boolean type.
data.example-usage-2#Example Usage:
You would usually use a tag instead of doing this! ex. tag IsRunning
component IsRunning = boolean
data.vectors#Vectors:
Vectors represent a vector in 3D space, most often as a point in 3D space.
They can be defined using the vector type.
data.bounding-vectors#Bounding Vectors:
The length (magnitude) of vectors can be bounded by placing a range within parenthesis after the type.
For example, if you wanted to create a unit vector (length between 0 and 1) you can do:
data.specifying-encoding#Specifying Encoding:
Vectors can be passed a number type within angular brackets after the type, to be used for encoding. For example, an i16 vector can be defined like so:
type VectorI16 = vector<i16>
Since Luau stores vectors as three f32s internally, any encoding larger than a f32 (ex. f64) will have no real effect on the numerical precision of the vector.
data.example-usage-3#Example Usage:
type Position = vector
type UnitVector = vector(0..1)
type VectorU8 = vector<u8>
 
component Position = Position
data.bounding-maps#Bounding Maps:
The number of elements in a map can be bounded by placing a range within parenthesis after the type.
For example, if you wanted to bound the number of elements between 1 and 100 you can do:
map StringToNumber = {[string]: f64}(1..100)
data.enums#Enums: Daisy supports two types of enums, unit and tagged enums
data.structs#Structs:
Structs are a fixed set of statically named keys and values.
They can be defined using the struct type. For example, a struct representing a theoretical game entity would look like:
type Entity = struct(
    Health = u8(0..100),
    Position = vector,
    Rotation = u16
)
Structs can be used inline anywhere a type expression is valid:
component Transform = struct(Position = vector, Rotation = CFrame)
Fields can be marked optional with ?:
type Config = struct(Name = string, Level = u8?, Active = boolean)
data.unknowns#Unknowns:
The unknown type represents values which cannot be known until runtime.
For unions, use tagged enums instead.
type Mystery = unknown
data.instances#Instances:
Daisy supports Roblox instances. They can be defined using the Instance type.
component Character = Instance
For Networking:
If a non-optional instance results in nil on the receiving side, it will raise a deserialization error, and the rest of the data will be dropped. Instances turning nil can be caused by many things, for example: instance streaming, instances that only exist on the sender's side, etc.
If you plan on replicating instances that might not exist, you must mark them optional.
data.specifying-class#Specifying Class:
Instances can be further narrowed by specifying their class, for example:
component Player = Instance(Player)
Subclasses of the specified class will also be accepted.
For example, an Instance typed as BasePart will also accept a Part.
data.cframes#CFrames:
CFrames represent a point and rotation about it in 3D space, most often as a point in 3D space. They can be defined using the CFrame type.
type Location = CFrame
data.specifying-encoding-1#Specifying Encodin:
CFrames can be passed two number types within angular brackets after the type, to be used for the positional and rotational encoding respectively. For example, a CFrame which uses an i16 for it's positional encoding and a f16 for it's rotational encoding can be defined like so:
type MyCFrame = CFrame<i16, f16>
The numerical precision limitations of vectors also apply to CFrames. 
Furthermore, using an integer type for the rotational encoding will result in the rotation being zeroed out.
data.other-roblox-types#Other Roblox Types:
Daisy also supports a handful of other Roblox types like:
BrickColor
Color3
DateTime
DateTimeMillis
